task: I’m reviewing a purchase offer on a small property and need a practical checklist for the money side before I sign. What items would you typically verify or include so the deal is documented cleanly and the closing process doesn’t get messy?

turns.agent_response:
Here’s a practical **money-side closing checklist** I’d use before signing a small property purchase offer, focused on keeping the deal clean and fully documented.

## 1) Price and economics
- Purchase price
- Earnest money amount, where it’s held, and whether it’s refundable
- Deposit deadlines and any additional deposit dates
- Closing date
- Prorations: taxes, rent, utilities, security deposits, HOA/condo dues if any
- Seller credits, concessions, or repair credits
- Any buyer closing cost contribution from seller
- Assignment rights, if relevant

## 2) Income verification
- Current rent roll
- Lease copies for every unit
- Actual bank deposits for the last 6–12 months
- T-12 operating statement
- Year-to-date income and expense report
- Any delinquency report
- Security deposit schedule
- Other income: laundry, parking, pet fees, storage, RUBS, late fees

## 3) Expense verification
- Property tax bills
- Insurance declarations page and premium history
- Utility bills by type for at least 12 months
- Maintenance and repair invoices
- Payroll or management fees, if applicable
- Pest, trash, landscaping, snow, security, and common area costs
- HOA or special assessment statements
- Any recurring vendor contracts
- Proof of paid expenses where possible, not just a seller summary

## 4) CapEx and deferred maintenance
- CapEx history with invoices
- Roof, HVAC, plumbing, electrical, foundation, parking, windows, appliances, and sewer history
- Open repair items and estimates
- Any recent work that still has warranty coverage
- Confirm what is included in the sale: appliances, fixtures, equipment, materials

## 5) Debt and financing
- Loan terms and lender requirements
- DSCR at current terms
- DSCR under stress assumptions
- Interest rate, amortization, term, prepay, and reserves
- Required down payment and cash to close
- Any assumed debt payoff or seller financing terms
- Escrow/impound requirements
- Reserve requirements after closing

## 6) Cash-to-close breakdown
- Purchase price
- Less earnest money credit
- Less seller credits
- Plus closing costs
- Plus lender fees
- Plus title/escrow fees
- Plus recording/transfer taxes
- Plus prepaid insurance
- Plus prepaid interest
- Plus initial reserve funding
- Plus any immediate repair or CapEx funding

## 7) Legal and title money items
- Title commitment reviewed
- Exceptions and liens identified
- Payoff statements for existing loans
- Tax lien or judgment search
- Mechanic’s lien exposure
- HOA estoppel, if applicable
- UCC filings on equipment, if relevant
- Confirm seller authority to sell and sign

## 8) Tenant and deposit handling
- Security deposit transfer amount
- Rent prorations at closing
- Any prepaid rents
- Tenant ledger reconciliation
- Move-in fees or deposits owed to third parties
- Written confirmation of who receives what at closing

## 9) Deal protection items
- Inspection contingency deadlines
- Financing contingency deadlines
- Title review deadline
- Right to terminate if material financials don’t reconcile
- Access to updated records before closing
- Final walk-through and condition confirmation

## 10) Clean documentation package
Ask for a file that includes:
- Executed purchase agreement
- Rent roll
- T-12
- Bank statements or deposit support
- Lease audit notes
- Tax and insurance docs
- Utility bills
- CapEx invoices
- Seller disclosures
- Title commitment
- Closing statement draft
- Payoff statements
- Any side letters or addenda

## 11) My practical reconciliation rule
For each major line item, force three questions:
1. What did the seller say?
2. What do the documents show?
3. What does it become under my assumptions?

That keeps you from underwriting off a polished summary that doesn’t match the source records.

## 12) Final pre-sign check
Before signing, make sure you can answer:
- What is the true cash needed to close?
- What is the adjusted NOI?
- What is the DSCR at current terms?
- What is the DSCR under stress?
- What is the worst-case 12-month downside?
- What items are still unverified?

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page offer review checklist** or a **spreadsheet-style grid** you can use deal by deal.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, this is solid. Not fluffy, actual checklist stuff. I’d use it.

A couple things I’d want in there too, just so the money side doesn’t get sloppy:
- seller’s closing payoff statement, not just a verbal balance
- proof of who actually owns the security deposits and any prepaid rent
- any open tax bill / supplemental tax exposure if the county reassesses after transfer
- transfer tax / recording fees spelled out line by line
- utility reimbursement or RUBS true-up, if that’s part of the income
- confirmation there are no hidden service contracts that auto-renew and hit the new owner

And I’d want the PSA to say the seller has to deliver a clean estoppel-style package on rents, deposits, delinquencies, and any side deals. If they can’t document it, I don’t credit it.

The reconciliation rule is the part that matters most. Seller story, documents, and my numbers. If those three don’t line up, I treat it as a problem, not a rounding error.
